data.title: Oracle: Still Priced Like It's Dead Money
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (ORCL) may be the only stock in software where I have a high conviction of a turnaround. In fact, despite the negative free cash flow and the funding needs, my conviction is higher than in Microsoft.

These are the only two names I own in the infamous software industry. Yes, the same industry I said I'm avoiding.

Why?

It is my view that the market is perceiving this stock as a cloud business, given its $553B RPO, even if software sales accounted for 36% of total revenue in the last quarter. Therefore, the pessimism from the AI disruption fear is unlikely to pressure the stock for too long.

As I'm about to discuss in this piece, financing doesn't make me sweat either, given how oversubscribed its latest funding rounds were. In fact, the spike in CDS makes complete sense to me, as I believe the same institutions that participate in the funding rounds buy these CDS as a hedge.

My concern has to do with the execution of the data center buildout. The Street is projecting a constant increase in the YOY revenue growth rate in each quarter for the next two years. Any delays could push revenue to the right, and likely lead to a selloff, similar to CoreWeave post Q3 2025 earnings.

That's the main risk that I see.

Is that enough to scare me off and keep me on the sidelines?

Not really. In fact, I own this stock in my portfolio. As for my price target, I don't think the stock will double before the end of the year. That said, mid-$200s is not far-fetched. Below, I provide my rationale

Rerating Is Not Far-Fetched

Let's start with the last earnings results (Q3 FY26), as I believe the earnings story caught skeptics by surprise.

Oracle reported Q3 revenue of $17.19 billion, beating the $16.91 billion analyst estimate. On the bottom line, the Q4 adjusted EPS guidance of $1.96 - $2.00 topped the $1.94 consensus.

Looking at the Street's revenue models for the next 4 quarters, I see a clear upward revision after the Q3 results, particularly for Q2 and Q3 FY 2027.

Why am I mentioning the Street's models? Well, simply because for a growth stock like Oracle, the share price tends to follow the 1-year earnings trend.

The disconnect shown in the chart above looks quite promising.

So, why now?

Technical analysis is often helpful, and the chart below looks promising after a strong bounce on a long-term uptrend. But most investors call it astrology (with good reasons), so I will stick to fundamentals-based arguments.

AI demand is exceeding supply. Don't take it from me. Take it from CEO Clay Magouyrk in one of his post-earnings blog posts:

Both are growing extremely quickly – Multicloud Database revenue grew 531% YoY. AI infrastructure revenue grew 243% YoY. Both also have demand that exceeds supply and a clear execution plan from Oracle that will rapidly turn that demand into profitable recurring revenue.

Those triple-digit growth figures in multicloud database and AI infrastructure revenue are quite compelling. In fact, based on the Street's revenue models, the growth story of Oracle is just in its early stages.

Did you notice in the chart above the acceleration in growth? Each quarter, the revenue growth is projected to increase from the low 20% in Q4 FY26 to the low 50% in Q4 FY28.

In fact, the company raised its FY27 revenue target to $90B, above the $86.6 billion analyst estimate. This outperformance is naturally driven by the cloud segment, which was up 44% yoy in the past quarter, accounting for 52% of total sales.

Software, on the other hand, accounted for 36% of total sales, but its topline grew by only 3% in Q3. Disappointing, to say the least.

That said, I think the investor focus is mainly on its cloud business. How am I so certain?

Well, Oracle exited Q3 with $553B in RPO, up 325% yoy. Unfortunately, the company hasn't disclosed how much of that RPO is cloud-related. Back in Q4 FY25, the (then) CEO, Safra Ada Catz, mentioned that cloud RPO represented nearly 80% of total RPO. Therefore, I don't think it's a stretch to assume that most of the backlog is cloud-related.

So what?

Well, the AI data center buildout is painfully slow, with lead times between 2 and 3 years for large AI data centers from concept to commissioning. By the way, that timeline can easily extend to 4+ years depending on power availability.

As a quick side note, that's why I'm bullish on GE Vernova and Bloom Energy. In fact, Oracle announced this week an expansion of its earlier partnership with Bloom Energy, as I believe the company is struggling to secure power from utilities.

Back to my point. This means that the RPO is going to take a good time until it converts into sales. If you are curious about the precise conversion metrics, I include below an excerpt from the latest 10-Q:

Remaining performance obligations were $552.6 billion as of February 28, 2026, of which we expect to recognize approximately 12% as revenues over the next twelve months, 31% over the subsequent month 13 to month 36, 35% over the subsequent month 37 to month 60 and the remainder thereafter.

The Financing Issue

Let's put things into perspective.

In the last earnings release, the company disclosed $48.25B in capital expenditures in the last 12 months. Interestingly, management only expects $50B in FY26 CapEx. That's not a major increase from the TTM spending.

That said, free cash flow was negative $24.7B in the last 12 months.

The trend in FCF is anything but encouraging.

In fact, the lack of FCF is the main reason the company is effectively taking a break with share buybacks (even though they haven't formally announced a pause).

That negative free cash flow is the big difference between Oracle and the hyperscalers, at least for now.

However, the situation is not as raw as the bears think. Let me explain.

Back in Q3, the company said:

Most of the increase in RPO in Q3 related to large scale AI contracts where Oracle does not expect to have to raise any incremental funds to support these contracts as most of the equipment needed is either funded upfront via customer prepayments so Oracle can purchase the GPUs, or the customer buys the GPUs and supplies them to Oracle.

That statement is key, as it suggests that funding is not scaling with the increase in RPO, as customers either prepay for equipment or provide the GPUs themselves, at least with the contracts signed in the last quarter.

Now, if we look at the funding plans for this year, the company's original financing plan called for $45 billion to $50 billion of 2026 gross proceeds, including up to $20 billion through an ATM equity program. So far, the company has raised $30B in a "substantially oversubscribed" round back in February.

By how much?

Well, $25B out of the $30B in financing came from bonds. According to some sources, Oracle’s $25B debt raise on February 2 drew more than $129B of orders. That implies the bond tranche was oversubscribed by more than $104 billion, or a little over 5.1x.

There is more.

Back in September last year (prior to the selloff), the company raised $18B in a round that peaked at $88B. So, it was oversubscribed by roughly $70 billion, or about 4.9x.

Therefore, I am not sweating about the spike in CDS.

Why? Simply because some underwriters with lending relationships to Oracle routinely hedge credit exposure, including purchasing credit default swaps or creating short positions. Don't take it from me. Take it from Oracle's February 2 prospectus supplement filing:

If any of the underwriters or their affiliates has a lending relationship with us, certain of those underwriters or their affiliates routinely hedge, certain other of those underwriters or their affiliates have hedged and are likely in the future to hedge or otherwise reduce their exposure, and certain other of those underwriters or their affiliates may hedge, their credit exposure to us consistent with their customary risk management policies. Typically, these underwriters and their affiliates would hedge or otherwise reduce such exposure by entering into transactions which consist of either the purchase of credit default swaps or the creation of short positions in our securities, including potentially the Depositary Shares offered hereby. Any such credit default swaps or short positions could adversely affect future trading prices of the Depositary Shares offered hereby.

Therefore, the spike in CDS doesn't make me sweat, as I strongly believe it's the effect of hedging. As for short interest, it's barely 1%, so I'm not sweating either.

What Makes Me Sweat

About a month ago, some sources reported that Oracle and OpenAI had dropped the planned Abilene expansion after financing talks dragged on. It seems that the extension was initially projected to be around 600 MW, so it's not a drop in the ocean.

Just a few days back, Reuters reported that OpenAI paused its main UK data center project due to regulation and high-energy costs.

To be clear, I am not surprised by the headline above, given the high bureaucracy on the other side of the Atlantic.

What makes me sweat, though, is that the market seems to believe the AI data center buildout will go smoothly. I have my doubts. And I am not the only one.

I am noticing an increasing number of analysts who argue that AI infrastructure spending is running into physical constraints that can slow or delay data center deployments.

Any delays could push Oracle’s revenue out to the right, which would likely trigger a selloff. Just remember what happened with CoreWeave shares after announcing back in November 2025 delays with its AI data center buildout due to a key data center partner.

Valuation

Oracle is cheap. Very cheap, in fact.

On a 1-year forward basis, the stock is trading at 21x next year's earnings.

That's basically cheaper than Nvidia (23x forward EPS). When compared to the other hyperscalers, Oracle is the cheapest in the group.

Yes, the multiple has compressed since the September 2025 highs. However, the acceleration in revenue growth is likely to rerate this stock back to grotesquely overvalued territory.

Looking Ahead

I think the market focus is shifting toward execution and delivery. Given how oversubscribed the previous funding rounds were, I don't think the company will face issues with finding capital (or paying down debt), at least in the near term.

I expect free cash flows to remain negative for the very long term, despite the margin upside guided in the last quarter. To be specific, management said that renting out third party GPU capacity should earn roughly 30%-40% margins, while database-related cloud services can carry 60%-80% gross margins. Decent, but I don't think we will see positive FCF in a very long time.

What makes me sweat is any delay in the AI data center buildout. That would push revenue to the right (think CoreWeave's Q3 2025 earnings), which will likely lead to a selloff.

In the absence of evidence (which is not the same as evidence of absence), I prefer to take the risk and own this name in my portfolio.

data.title: Warrior Met Coal: A Low-Cost Premium Coking Coal Producer
data.description:
Warrior Met Coal, Inc. (HCC) is a manufacturer of hard coking coal. This is largely utilized in steelmaking industries, those operating on a non-thermal basis. HCC produces premium-quality coal, which allows it to secure higher sales realizations from the product. This is the Seeking Alpha summary for the company.

Warrior Met Coal, Inc. is a U.S.-based company primarily engaged in the production and export of non-thermal steelmaking coal, also known as hard coking coal (HCC). The company's headquarters are located in Brookwood, Alabama, and it operates several underground mines, including Mine No. 4, Mine No. 7, and the recently developed Blue Creek mine. Warrior Met Coal serves the global steel industry, with its products being a critical component in steel production for metal manufacturers in Europe, South America, and Asia. The company's operations are characterized by efficient longwall mining techniques, which contribute to its position as a large-scale, low-cost producer. Warrior Met Coal also engages in the extraction and sale of natural gas as a byproduct of its coal operations. Incorporated in 2015, Warrior Met Coal has strategically focused on producing premium quality steelmaking coal, which allows it to secure higher realized prices and operating margins compared to other U.S. producers. The Blue Creek mine, which commenced operations in October 2025, is expected to significantly enhance the company's production capacity and cost structure, making it a key asset in Warrior's long-term growth strategy

At present the market capitalization stands at around $4.6 billion with a share price of ~88 and per share book value of $~40.7. The company is virtually debt-free with liquidity of ~400 million. This is important, since unlike many other commodity players with higher leverage, HCC can, to a large extent, absorb the pricing pressures of its produce. In addition, HCC (Warrior Met Coal) is one of the lowest-cost producers of premium-quality coal. This not only puts it in a better position with reference to many other players, but its product is sold at close to index prices of coal.

For the year ended FY25, the company met and exceeded almost all of its guidance. These are the metrics that are in management’s control. Of course, the eventual sales realizations remain subject to the market. With a total production of ~10.2 million tons, HCC remains one of the larger coal producers in the United States.

Blue Creek—A Significant Development

Blue Creek marks a significant expansion for the HCC. In the company's own words, it is the most significant organic investment for the company. Fortunately for the investors, the developments at the said mine have been satisfactory and have exceeded expectations, at least till the time being. The company was able to advance its production timeline by eight months ahead of the stated schedule.

The company had spent around ~$956.8 million on Blue Creek so far and had been able to produce ~2.0 million st for FY end 2025 from the said mine. Kindly note that virtually all of the capex utilised for Blue Creek was from internal retained earnings and cash flows. With the operational commencement of Blue Creek Longwall, the production targets for the company are meaningfully enhanced.

The FY 26 expected production is over ~12 million, which, considering the company's recent guidance track record, would probably be met. In addition, Blue Creek happens to be a very low-cost mine globally. At the same time, execution remains the key, and in addition, the coal produced from Blue Creek mines would slightly decrease the overall sales realization vs. index price for HCC. The company acknowledges the same as well; however, the increase in production volume largely offsets the impact.

Metallurgical Coal—A Structural Distinction and Steel-Driven Demand

This is a key point to keep in mind while analysing HCC. There is a clear distinction between thermal coal, which is utilised for the production of electricity, and coking coal, also known as metallurgical coal. While narratives around clean energy and energy transition, etc., keep on happening at present, even if they remain intact, they would tend to impact thermal coal at large. Metallurgical coal, in contrast, remains a critical raw material in blast furnace steelmaking, where scalable substitutes are still limited.

At the same time, demand for steelmaking remains robust as well. Steel remains a key pillar in the global economy, with applications across infrastructure, transport, and capital goods, including for infrastructural development necessary for clean energy transmission as well as a matter of fact. Obviously, these are forecasted numbers but are sufficient to reflect the expected increase in steel production. Also, steel is predominantly made using metallurgical coal (coking coal) in blast furnaces, with approximately 70% to 72% of total global steel production relying on this coal-based method of steel production.

India naturally warrants a separate mention and emerges as a key driver of incremental steel demand, leading to increased demand for premium-quality coking coal. For context, a close peer, Alpha Metallurgical Resources (AMR), exports around ~39% of its total sales to India. The purpose of this discussion is not to deviate into steel but to highlight two aspects. First, the ongoing energy transition is unlikely to materially impact metallurgical coal demand in the foreseeable future. Second, steel demand growth will inadvertently support sustained demand for coking coal, aiding both volume absorption and long-term structural pricing support for metallurgical coal.

This is another outlook from the EIA directly reflecting metallurgical coal consumption. There is significant potential apparent in India there as well. However, on a global basis, overall demand CAGR stands slightly negative and thus does not suggest strong aggregate growth. That said, an important offset exists: Warrior produces premium-quality met coal, unlike many other global producers. In addition, there is a possibility of closures of high-cost mines and limited new greenfield supply. Notably, Warrior Met Coal remains one of the lowest-cost producers globally.

The expected decline in new coal supply, largely due to depletion of older mines, including shutdowns of high-cost operations, which, in the present environment, are not able to operate at lower coal price levels, tends to benefit players like HCC. The long-term demand, at least for premium coal producers such as HCC, still remains largely intact, while supply is possibly declining from 2030. This naturally creates a dual-layered structural advantage for companies like HCC, at least for the foreseeable future.

A Comment on Valuations and Metallurgical Coal Price Trajectory

This section is also important for HCC because the stock saw a significant appreciation over the last year and now seems to trade at a hefty trailing multiple. However, before concluding the counter to be expensive, it should also be noted that current multiples are backward-looking in the present case.

The company was able to make ~$230 million in cash flow from operations for FY25. It is to be noted, however, that the sales realizations were not particularly favourable for FY25. The average selling price was around ~$146 for FY25.

Considering both the increase in production volume for FY26, along with a possibly higher sales realization than the previous year, there is a meaningfully higher potential for cash generation in FY26. At the present coking coal price of ~$220, if that level were to persist for the entire year, Warrior would possibly generate over $500 million in cash flow, with revenue of over $2 billion. (The guided production and sale for FY 26 is ~13 MM tons.) Compared with the current market capitalization of $4.5 billion, this implies a significant cash flow yield. Kindly note that the company is incurring substantial capex, largely in the form of growth rather than maintenance, and therefore the free cash flow might appear low in a formulaic sense. However, when segregating maintenance vs. growth capex, the cash flow and earning power of the company, with reference to the current equity market cap, are significant.

It is also to be highlighted that the current Metallurgical Coal prices, especially when adjusted for inflation, appear to be rather moderate. Although we cannot know for sure what the prices of the coal will actually be, still, however, considering that current prices are not only at moderate levels but, when analysed with reference to inflation and other commodity prices, appear relatively lower to moderate structurally. I have already emphasized that met coal differs from thermal coal and demand drivers remain intact, especially for companies like Warrior producing a premium-quality product. Any increase in price realizations would directly result in significant cash flow to the company and enhance the valuation case.

That said, however, HCC eventually is a commodity producer, and anything could happen with the eventual prices of the underlying commodity, and a decline, however low the probability might be, would impact the profitability and cash flows for the company. This is the proposition an investor would be getting themselves into. The commodity nature of the produce naturally puts limits on any analysis that could be provided, which I would come to next.

Risks and Limitations to Buy Thesis

As I highlighted in the previous paragraph, eventually HCC is a commodity producer whose prices are contingent on the market. There is no guarantee of the outcome. The company can control cost elements as best as it might, but still a prolonged depression in coking coal prices can disrupt the thesis. Further, prices below ~$150–175 would likely pressure cash flows and could possibly turn free cash flows negative. Being considered a cyclical player, near-term price compression also results in the contraction of market multiples offered to the company, which causes share price declines as well. Further, a recent appreciation of ~100% has naturally reduced the inherent margin of safety in the investment. In addition, since the company currently sells close to the PLV index prices, any widening of the spreads needs to be watched out for, especially when volume increases from production and Blue Creek execution occur. Also, in case the coal price weakens, the large recent investment and expansion of Blue Creek could amplify the downside.

Therefore, an investor whose thresholds of value and prudence are still higher might consider keeping the stock on the watchlist at the moment.

In Conclusion

At present, at a price of $88, HCC is rated a buy, but only for those investors who are willing to be exposed to commodity price risks. The probability-weighted expectancy remains positive on the investment, but unlike durable moats, the company is inherently exposed to factors outside its control for the key element, which is realizations and sale prices. The recent expansion to Blue Creek, if executed right, offers a significant value accretion to the shareholders and also remains a key development to be watched. For enterprising investors with adequate risk appetite, therefore, HCC still remains something to be watched closely.

data.title: Globant SA: Rating Downgrade On Potential Revenue Growth Deceleration
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Globant SA (GLOB) as I expected the market to re-rate multiples upward as GLOB would start to show growth acceleration. I still think the recovery story works, but it is taking longer, and the setup is less clean now. Bookings and pipeline have improved, which is encouraging, but the key issue has shifted to conversion. Larger deals are still taking time to ramp, and the AI transition may weigh on headline revenue growth.

The recovery story

My idea of GLOB’s equity story was that growth should improve over the next two years as demand got better, clients started spending again, and AI became a bigger part of the story. I also thought that there was enough evidence to convince the market, be it via backlog strength, delivery capacity, or expansion into new areas. The broader recovery angle is still there, but the underlying support has shifted from a broad recovery in spending to more company-specific. The good news is that the path to improvement is more visible now, as the expected growth re-acceleration is tied to projects that are already signed and still need to ramp up. But at the same time, it also makes execution more important. If the ramp-up is slower than expected, then revenue growth recovery gets pushed out again.

The more important change, though, is AI. I framed AI as part of the upside case, as we lacked proof that it could be meaningful. That is not the case today. GLOB is now actively pushing AI-pods with a pricing model based on outcomes or usage instead of traditional time-and-materials work. And this is huge because it shows us that AI not only can help to capture demand, but it could also improve the economics of the business. Sure, this new AI model may come at lower pricing, but lower pricing for clients does not automatically mean a worse outcome for GLOB if margins are better and the work becomes less tied to labor. This actually extends the growth runway and GLOB’s ability to scale as more workloads are being handled by AI agents.

There is the pushback that AI could weaken the role of firms like GLOB. For context, the idea is that if clients decide they no longer need as much outside engineering help because AI lets them build, test, and maintain software with fewer people. It could also pressure pricing for GLOB, since outcome-based AI work may carry less revenue than the old labor-heavy model. On top of that, hyperscalers and software vendors are pushing their own AI tools directly to enterprises, which increases the risk that some work gets pulled in-house or bypasses firms like GLOB entirely. But I don’t think it is as bad as it seems, at least for the foreseeable future. My view is that clients still need help putting different models, tools, and platforms together into something that actually works. On top of that, the higher-end technical work and domain knowledge are still not easy to bring in-house. I agree the risk is still there, but I don’t think it structurally impairs near-term growth.

Why I am staying neutral instead of staying bullish

The story today is no longer about whether GLOB can win more demand. Bookings are there, and the pipeline is bigger. The problem is conversion. Larger deals are still taking time to ramp, and until I see that flowing through the P&L, I am not confident of a meaningful near-term acceleration in revenue. The idea here is that long conversion times create a real timing mismatch between deals won and reported growth. GLOB can be winning larger and more strategic work and still post weak growth numbers because the revenue comes through too slowly. That is why I do not think bookings alone are enough to support the stock here.

As for AI, I see the merits, but even here I think investors need to stay careful. I agree that the shift toward A.I. pods and a more outcome-based model should improve the quality of this business. Better margins and less reliance on labor would be a real positive. But that does not automatically mean faster revenue growth. In fact, lower pricing in the new model could be a drag on headline revenue growth even if the economics improve underneath. Mix is also an issue. If clients start moving work away from the old labor-heavy model into AI pods, it will naturally cause a deflationary impact on the top line at first. The faster the migration, the bigger the impact, albeit this is good for the long-term growth profile. The counterargument here is that GLOB may make that up through more wallet share, more AI-led use cases, and broader adoption. But there is a real risk that GLOB goes through a period where margins improve and the business position gets better, but revenue growth decelerates hard, and given how the stock valuation tracked in line with revenue growth expectations, multiples could go lower if growth decelerates.

Valuation

The outlook for GLOB has become a lot murkier vs. last November. While GLOB has seen record bookings and a bigger pipeline, the question is conversion here. Revenue is unlikely to see any major acceleration in the near term as larger deals are taking time to convert. GLOB leaning on AI may also help improve economics, but the lower pricing in the new model may weigh down on revenue growth even if it's offset by better margins. Management guidance is also pointing to low-single-digit growth in FY2026, which puts off hope for a V-shaped bounce. From a growth perspective, GLOB is the slowest-growing company vs. its peers, and for that reason, I think it's fair that this trades discounted. The current 7.3x forward PE seems right if we compare it to EPAM, which trades at 9.5x but has mid-single-digit 1-year forward growth.

Conclusion

My downgrade to hold for GLOB is because the long-term story is still intact, but the near-term growth outlook has become a lot murkier. It is clearly winning demand and building a more scalable AI-led model, but the revenue growth acceleration is not showing up yet. That matters to me because the stock’s multiple has historically moved with growth expectations. Until I see better conversion from backlog and a clearer acceleration in reported revenue, I do not think the multiple will shift up from here in the near term.

data.title: Big March Deals Push Total Outsize Transaction Value To Multiyear High In Q1
data.description:
There were 11 M&A deals worldwide with transaction values exceeding $10 billion in the first quarter. The period's total transaction value of $305.33 billion was the highest since the second quarter of 2019.

In March, there were four $10 billion-plus deals announced with a combined transaction value of $98.77 billion. Two of those deals ranked among the largest announced in the last 12 months, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

In the largest deal of the month, an investor group on March 2 announced its plan to purchase Virginia-based power company AES Corp. (AES). The sale has a deal value of $10.82 billion and a transaction value of $48.08 billion, which includes the deal value plus the value of assumed current liabilities. Based on the transaction value, it ranks as the fifth-largest deal announced in the past 12 months.

The investor group purchasing the power company includes BlackRock Inc. (BLK) subsidiary Global Infrastructure Management LLC, which was also a buyer in the $40 billion acquisition of Aligned Data Centers LLC, announced in October 2025. The takeover addresses a growing need for power driven by AI.

Zurich-based insurance company Zurich Insurance Group AG (ZURVY) on March 2 announced a definitive agreement to acquire London-based insurer Beazley PLC (BZLEY)(BZLYF) after two failed attempts. That deal has a transaction value of $10.84 billion.

Zurich Insurance Group sent Beazley its first offer Jan. 4, then followed up Jan. 19 and again on Feb. 4. Beazley's management rejected initial bids for undervaluing the company, according to a bid announcement. The final offer gives Beazley's shareholders 1,310 pence in cash per Beazley share and a dividend of 25 pence per Beazley share.

In the US life insurance sector, Houston-based Corebridge Financial Inc. (CRBG) said March 26 that it plans to acquire New York City-based Equitable Holdings Inc. (EQH) in a deal with a transaction value of $10.77 billion. The merger will fold Equitable's specialty finance business into Corebridge's larger retirement and insurance business and create a top US life insurance group, according to Market Intelligence data.

In another Texas-based deal, Houston-based Sysco Corp. (SYY) said March 30 that it plans to acquire Whitestone, New York-based Jetro Cash & Carry LLC, the parent company of Restaurant Depot LLC. The food distribution deal has a transaction value of $29.08 billion.

Sysco and Jetro Cash & Carry have complementary products and can streamline supplier pricing to improve combined profitability, Sysco Chairman and CEO Kevin Hourican said during an M&A call with analysts.

About this analysis

This feature classifies M&A deals with transaction values of more than $10 billion by their definitive agreement date. It excludes deal announcements or announced bids that have not reached a definitive agreement. Installments of this feature before March 2026 included all announced bids.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
